original_hypothesis.title: forceInclusion Missing Time-Based Delay Check Allows Premature Forced Message Inclusion
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: The forceInclusion function only enforces a block-number-based delay check (l1BlockAndTime[0] + delayBlocks_ >= block.number) but completely omits the time-based delay check. The error ForceIncludeTimeTooSoon is defined in Error.sol but never used anywhere in the codebase. The delaySeconds variable is stored and read in getTimeBounds/maxTimeVariationInternal but is not enforced during force inclusion. On chains where block production rate varies or accelerates, messages could be force-included before the intended time-based delay has expired.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a definitive code defect - the error type exists specifically for this check, the time delay parameters are stored and used elsewhere, and the dual block+time delay pattern is standard for Arbitrum SequencerInbox. The missing check weakens censorship resistance guarantees.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Only block-based check present: `if (l1BlockAndTime[0] + delayBlocks_ >= block.number) revert ForceIncludeBlockTooSoon()`. No timestamp check against delaySeconds. ForceIncludeTimeTooSoon error defined at Error.sol but never referenced.

preconditions: Chain has variable block production rate (block time != constant seconds)
Block production temporarily accelerates relative to wall-clock time
Delayed messages exist that haven't been included by the sequencer

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Missing complementary time-based check alongside block-based check
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: l1BlockAndTime[1] (timestamp) and delaySeconds are available but not used in the force inclusion path. The ForceIncludeTimeTooSoon error is defined but unreferenced.
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No `if (l1BlockAndTime[1] + delaySeconds_ >= block.timestamp) revert ForceIncludeTimeTooSoon()` check

analysis_questions: On which target chains (L1 Ethereum, Arbitrum L2) does block production rate vary enough to exploit this?
What is the maximum time-vs-block skew observable in practice?
Was the time check intentionally omitted or is this an oversight?
validation_requirements: Verify ForceIncludeTimeTooSoon is never used in the full codebase
Calculate worst-case premature force inclusion window under variable block rates
Check if the delay buffer mechanism compensates for this missing check

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: During a period of fast L1 block production, an attacker calls forceInclusion for a delayed message. The block number delay has been met (sufficient blocks elapsed), but the intended time delay has not yet expired. The message is force-included prematurely, potentially disrupting sequencer batch ordering or reducing the sequencer's exclusion window.
prerequisites: Variable block production rate on the host chain
Delayed messages pending in the bridge

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Reduced censorship resistance window; sequencer ordering disruption
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users relying on sequencer-only window for ordering guarantees
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Protocol continues operating but with weakened timing guarantees

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No test verifying time-based delay enforcement in forceInclusion

validation_result.mechanism: missing time-based delay check in forceInclusion
validation_result.impact_summary: The forceInclusion function lacks a timestamp-based delay check, allowing premature forced message inclusion on chains with variable block production rates. The ForceIncludeTimeTooSoon error is defined but never used.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The forceInclusion function in SequencerInbox.sol only enforces a block-number-based delay check (line ~244: `if (l1BlockAndTime[0] + delayBlocks_ >= block.number) revert ForceIncludeBlockTooSoon()`) but completely omits the complementary time-based delay check. The error `ForceIncludeTimeTooSoon` is defined in Error.sol (line ~152) with a descriptive comment `Force include can only read messages more seconds old than the delay period`, clearly indicating intent to use it. The `delaySeconds` state variable is stored, set via `_setMaxTimeVariation`, and used in `getTimeBounds()` for batch header time bounds, but is never checked in the force inclusion path. This is a definitive code defect where one half of a dual block+time delay enforcement pattern was implemented while the other half was left out.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Direct evidence from SequencerInbox.sol forceInclusion function shows only a block-based check at line 244. Error.sol defines ForceIncludeTimeTooSoon at line 152 which is imported in SequencerInbox.sol but never referenced in any function body. The delaySeconds variable is set in _setMaxTimeVariation (line ~601) and read in maxTimeVariationInternal (line ~208) and getTimeBounds (line ~176), but never used in forceInclusion. The missing check should be: `if (l1BlockAndTime[1] + delaySeconds_ >= block.timestamp) revert ForceIncludeTimeTooSoon();`

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Only block-based delay check is present. The complementary timestamp-based check using delaySeconds and ForceIncludeTimeTooSoon is completely absent. Both l1BlockAndTime[0] (block number) and l1BlockAndTime[1] (timestamp) are available as parameters, but only the block number is checked against delay requirements.

vulnerable_code_snippets.code_snippet:
/// @dev Force include can only read messages more seconds old than the delay period
error ForceIncludeTimeTooSoon();
vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: This error is defined specifically for the time-based force inclusion check but is never used anywhere in the codebase. Its existence alongside ForceIncludeBlockTooSoon (which IS used at SequencerInbox.sol:244) strongly indicates the time check was intended but omitted.

delaySeconds is stored and returned by this function, and it is used in getTimeBounds() for batch header validation. However, it is never fetched or used in forceInclusion to validate the timestamp delay.

calling_sequence: 1. External caller invokes forceInclusion with l1BlockAndTime[0] (block) and l1BlockAndTime[1] (timestamp)
2. Message hash reconstructed and validated against delayed inbox accumulator
3. delayBlocks_ computed (potentially via buffer for delay-bufferable case)
4. Block-based check: l1BlockAndTime[0] + delayBlocks_ >= block.number → revert ForceIncludeBlockTooSoon
5. MISSING: Time-based check: l1BlockAndTime[1] + delaySeconds_ >= block.timestamp → should revert ForceIncludeTimeTooSoon
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: After the block-based check at line 244, execution proceeds directly to the delayed inbox accumulator verification at lines 247-253. There is no time-based check between these points. The delaySeconds value is available via maxTimeVariationInternal() but is never called in this function. The l1BlockAndTime[1] parameter (timestamp) is available as a function parameter but only used for message hash computation, not for delay validation.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: The SequencerInboxStubCreator.js deployment script shows delayBlocks: 10000 and delaySeconds: 10000 configured together, confirming both are intended to be used. Deployment scripts don't reveal whether this is intentional omission.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: On L1 Ethereum (12-second blocks), the correlation between block numbers and timestamps is relatively stable, making the practical impact limited but non-zero during periods of faster block production. On L2 chains (Arbitrum) or custom chains where block production rates vary significantly, the impact increases. The attack scenario: during fast block production, delayBlocks worth of blocks pass before delaySeconds worth of time has elapsed. An attacker calls forceInclusion - the block check passes but the intended time delay hasn't expired. The message is force-included prematurely, reducing the sequencer's exclusive ordering window. This is a permissionless function requiring no capital.
validation_result.impact_assessment: The missing time-based check weakens censorship resistance guarantees by allowing premature force inclusion when block production rates diverge from expected timing. On L1 Ethereum, the practical window is small but non-zero. On L2 chains or chains with variable block times, the window could be significant. The defect reduces the dual-check safety margin that the protocol was designed with, as evidenced by the defined-but-unused error type and the stored-but-unchecked delaySeconds parameter.
recommended_mitigations: Add the missing time-based check in forceInclusion: fetch delaySeconds_ from maxTimeVariationInternal() and add `if (l1BlockAndTime[1] + delaySeconds_ >= block.timestamp) revert ForceIncludeTimeTooSoon();`
For delay-bufferable mode, consider whether a time-based buffer analog is needed alongside the block-based buffer
Add test coverage verifying both block AND time delay enforcement in forceInclusion
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy SequencerInbox with delayBlocks=100 and delaySeconds=1200. 2. Create a delayed message at block N, timestamp T. 3. Advance chain to block N+101 (passing block check) while ensuring wall-clock time is less than T+1200 (failing intended time check). 4. Call forceInclusion - it should succeed despite the time delay not having expired. 5. Verify the message was force-included prematurely.
uncertainties: Whether the omission was intentional for gas optimization or simplification, though the defined-but-unused error strongly suggests it was unintentional
The exact practical impact on L1 Ethereum given relatively stable ~12-second block times
Whether the delay buffer mechanism was intended to replace the time-based check (unlikely given the error definition)
additional_testing_needed: Grep full codebase (including files not provided) for any usage of ForceIncludeTimeTooSoon
Check git history for whether a time check was previously present and removed
Test on chains with variable block production rates to measure practical exploitation window
Verify behavior during Ethereum block time variance (e.g., during high MEV activity or missed slots)
